content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

When many people first enter the world of AI, they assume that success comes from building a better model. It is easy to understand why. News headlines constantly talk about new models, larger models, and record-breaking benchmarks. As a result, it is natural to think that the biggest challenge in AI is creating the model itself. As I continue learning AI Engineering, I have realized that for most organizations, the challenge is often something entirely different. The real challenge is choosing the right model for the right problem.
A few years ago, many machine learning projects required teams to train their own models. Today, developers have access to a growing number of foundation models through APIs, open-source releases, and commercial platforms. Instead of asking how to build a model, teams are increasingly asking which model they should use. This shift changes the role of AI Engineering from model creation to model selection and adaptation.
At first glance, choosing a model may seem straightforward. Why not simply use the model with the highest benchmark score? The problem is that benchmarks do not always reflect real-world requirements. A model that performs exceptionally well on a public leaderboard may not be the best choice for a specific application. Different organizations care about different things. Some prioritize accuracy. Others focus on latency, cost, privacy, or customization. The best model for one company may be completely unsuitable for another.
Consider a customer support application. One company might value response quality above everything else and be willing to accept higher costs. Another company may need to handle millions of requests every day and therefore prioritize efficiency and cost savings. Both companies are solving similar problems, yet they may choose entirely different models. This illustrates an important lesson: model selection is always tied to business requirements.
Another factor that influences model selection is data privacy. Many organizations work with sensitive information that cannot easily be shared with external providers. In such cases, using a hosted API may introduce concerns around compliance, governance, or security. Some organizations may decide to host models themselves to maintain greater control over their data. Others may determine that the convenience and capabilities of a managed service outweigh those concerns. There is no universal answer because every organization operates under different constraints.
Performance is another important consideration. Users expect AI applications to respond quickly. A highly capable model that takes several seconds to answer every request may create a poor user experience. On the other hand, a slightly less capable model that responds almost instantly might be more effective in practice. This reminds me of a common lesson in software engineering: the technically superior solution is not always the best solution. The best solution is often the one that balances performance, cost, reliability, and maintainability.
One of the most interesting ideas I encountered is that public benchmarks should be viewed as starting points rather than final answers. Benchmarks can help identify weak models and narrow the list of candidates, but they rarely determine the best choice for a specific application. Real-world evaluation requires testing models against actual business requirements. A model that excels at answering academic questions may perform very differently when generating customer emails, analyzing security incidents, or summarizing internal documents.
This lesson also reinforced the importance of evaluation. Selecting a model is not a one-time decision. Teams often need to compare multiple models, experiment with prompts, test retrieval strategies, and measure outcomes using meaningful metrics. Success depends less on finding a perfect model and more on creating a systematic process for evaluating options.
As a software developer, I found this perspective particularly valuable because it shifts the focus from chasing technology trends to solving business problems. The goal is not to use the most advanced model available. The goal is to use the model that best satisfies the requirements of the application. Sometimes that may be the latest frontier model. Other times it may be a smaller, cheaper, and faster alternative.
Another takeaway is that modern AI Engineering is increasingly about adaptation rather than creation. Developers spend more time experimenting with prompts, retrieval systems, evaluation pipelines, and application architecture than training foundation models from scratch. The availability of powerful pre-trained models has dramatically lowered the barrier to entry, allowing teams to focus more on delivering value and less on building core AI capabilities themselves.
My biggest takeaway from this lesson is simple. The question is no longer “Can we build a model?” The more important question is “Which model best helps us solve the problem?” In many cases, choosing the right model, evaluating it properly, and integrating it effectively will have a far greater impact on the success of an AI application than building a model from scratch. That realization highlights why model selection has become one of the most important skills in modern AI Engineering.
